topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like a physical insult in my hands. I’d found it tucked inside a discarded textbook at the campus library—my own essay, perfectly formatted, glowing under the harsh fluorescent lights of the study carrels. But it wasn't mine. It was published. A snippet of my deeply personal narrative, complete with my chosen university’s crest at the bottom, sitting right next to a glowing review of a philosophy major. My jaw tightened until a dull ache radiated through my temples. I crumpled the paper slowly, deliberately, the sound of the tearing sound echoing too loudly in the otherwise silent archive. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping back against the linoleum floor, and walked out of the library without looking back, the adrenaline making my hands tremble slightly.

[story 2]

My breath hitched in my throat, a tight, shallow thing. When I finally unfolded the document, the words swam before my eyes, mocking me with their eloquence. It was my voice, my unfiltered thoughts about navigating loss and finding resilience, now polished, curated, and displayed as some sort of aspirational benchmark. A hot flush spread across my neck and cheeks. I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles bleached white. "This is impossible," I whispered, the sound rough and unfamiliar. I paced the small room, my movements jerky and agitated, kicking at a stray binder. Every glance toward the door felt like a spotlight, and I couldn't seem to settle my gaze on anything for fear of seeing the evidence again.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen, the light reflecting off the glass, making my eyes water. The essay, my confession, my carefully constructed attempt at articulating who I was, was there, sitting under a headline promising intellectual depth. A deep, internal churning started in my gut, a pressurized feeling that demanded an immediate release. I slammed my fist onto the wooden surface of the table, the impact jarring my arm. I leaned back, sucking in a ragged breath, my chest rising and falling rapidly. I began to pace the length of my cramped apartment, my steps quick and uneven, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic rhythm of my pulse. I needed to destroy it.
